RR must balance compression (generalizing) with particularization (attending to unique features) — a trade-off managed across all cognitive domains.
General intelligence (Spearman's g) is best understood as the capacity for cross-domain management of the compression–particularization trade-off, i.e., as RR.
RR is inherently developmental: increased fitness produces complexification, generating new cognitive levels — genuine self-transcendence.
Embodiment and embeddedness are constitutive of RR, not merely enabling conditions.

title: RR, the Brain, Insight, and Consciousness
essence: Episode 32 moves from the abstract theory of relevance realization to its proposed neural implementation and then to its role in two of the most challenging phenomena in cognitive science: insight and consciousness. For the implementation, Vervaeke draws on the science of complex systems, proposing that the brain operates at or near a point of self-organized criticality (SOC) — the state poised between order and randomness that maximizes information integration and sensitivity to perturbation. Such a critical system naturally generates small-world networks (SWN): highly clustered local modules with sparse long-range connections that allow rapid global coordination. The episode's central claim is that a moment of insight — the sudden restructuring of a problem — corresponds precisely to a phase transition in which the brain's current connectivity pattern (operating in a regular, clustered regime) is disrupted and the system briefly enters a small-world configuration that integrates previously unconnected information clusters. On consciousness, Vervaeke argues that its core function is to perform relevance realization within perspectival knowing: consciousness is not the seat of all cognition but the workspace where the organism calibrates what to pay attention to, binding agent and arena into a coherent salience landscape. Heidegger's notion of caring is invoked to show that this calibration is always already interested, participatory, and existentially loaded.
keyClaims: The brain operates at or near self-organized criticality, which maximizes information integration and the capacity for rapid global coordination.
Small-world network topology — clustered modules with sparse long-range hubs — is the structural signature of a brain optimized for RR.
Insight is a phase transition from regular to small-world connectivity, allowing previously unconnected information clusters to be rapidly integrated.
The primary function of consciousness is relevance realization within perspectival knowing — setting the criterion for what is salient.
Caring, in Heidegger's sense, is constitutive of the RR that underwrites consciousness: cognition is always already interested.

title: The Spirituality of RR: Wonder, Awe, and the Sacred
essence: Episode 33 makes the decisive move from cognitive science to spirituality by arguing that religio — a term Vervaeke now introduces as the name for the spiritual dimension of relevance realization — is not a mere add-on to cognition but its natural culmination. Religio names the higher-order relevance realization by which the organism becomes sensitive not to this or that feature of the environment, but to the very conditions and ground of its own meaning-making. This is experienced not as focal perception but as non-focal disclosure: the felt sense that something of inexhaustible depth is being touched, which manifests in the three characteristic modes of wonder, awe, and mystery. Wonder is the disclosure of inexhaustibility in what is positive and generative; awe blends admiration with a sense of being diminished before something larger; mystery is the sense that one can never fully capture what is being disclosed. Drawing on Geertz's analysis of religion as a meta-meaning frame and Schleiermacher's theology of feeling, Vervaeke situates these experiences within the broader ecology of worldview attunement: religio is what sustains and renews the worldview, preventing it from collapsing into mere habit or ideology. The episode ends with a preview of Otto's numinous as the next conceptual tool for understanding how sacredness generates both attraction and terror.
keyClaims: Religio is higher-order RR: the organism's sensitivity to the very ground and conditions of its own meaning-making.
Wonder, awe, and mystery are the characteristic non-focal modes of religio — they disclose inexhaustibility rather than content.
Religion, in Geertz's sense, provides the meta-meaning frame within which all other meanings take their significance — and religio is the cognitive machinery behind this.
Schleiermacher's absolute dependence points to the same structural feature as religio: the organism's participatory relation to what it cannot fully represent.
Otto's numinous — introduced at the episode's close — will illuminate why religio generates both fascination and horror.

title: Sacredness, Horror, Music, and the Symbol
essence: Episode 34 unfolds the dialectical structure of the experience of sacredness by developing Otto's concept of the numinous: the sacred discloses itself as simultaneously fascinating and horrifying — mysterium tremendum et fascinans — because it overwhelms the organism's capacity to assimilate while simultaneously drawing it forward into transformation. Vervaeke analyses this as an opponent processing dynamic: meta-assimilation (the pull toward the sacred) and meta-accommodation (the disruption of one's current frame) are locked in a productive tension that generates sustained anagoge. Horror, in this framework, is not a failure of the sacred but an essential component of it — the experience of the inter-categorical, of what violates the conceptual containers through which we organize experience (drawing on Mary Douglas's analysis of the abominable). Music then enters as the clearest non-linguistic exemplar of this dynamic: music is serious play with our relevance-realization machinery itself, generating the meta-assimilation/meta-accommodation dynamic through pure relational structure without reference to objects. This leads to the episode's culminating concept — the symbol — which Vervaeke distinguishes from a mere sign (which represents by convention) and from a metaphor (which maps between two existing frames): a symbol participates in what it discloses, enacting a structural resonance between the organism's RR and the inexhaustible depth of what is being disclosed.
keyClaims: The numinous (Otto) is the phenomenological core of sacredness: simultaneously fascinating and horrifying, it engages opponent processing between meta-assimilation and meta-accommodation.
Horror is not the absence of the sacred but its necessary shadow — the experience of what ruptures categorical containment.
Music is serious play with RR machinery: it generates meta-assimilation/meta-accommodation dynamically without propositional content.
A symbol is not a sign (conventional representation) nor merely a metaphor: it participates in what it discloses through structural resonance.
The symbol–RR relationship is one of mutual activation: symbols work by exapting existing cognitive patterns and pointing them toward inexhaustible depth.

title: The Symbol, Sacredness, and the Sacred
essence: Episode 35 completes the theory of the symbol and uses it to arrive at a rigorously cognitive-scientific account of the sacred. Vervaeke develops the symbol as an EPIC structure — Ecstatic, Participatory, Integrative, and Complex — each dimension corresponding to a distinct cognitive function. Ecstasy: the symbol carries the organism beyond its current salience landscape into an expanded mode of contact with reality. Participatory: the symbol engages the organism's participatory knowing rather than merely conveying propositional content — the participant is changed by the engagement. Integrative: the symbol unifies multiple levels of knowing simultaneously — procedural, perspectival, and participatory — in a single enactive gesture. Complex: the symbol draws on an inexhaustible depth of structural resonance that can never be fully exhausted by any analysis. Drawing on Polanyi's distinction between subsidiary and focal awareness, Vervaeke shows how the symbol works by making the machinery of RR itself the medium of contact with the inexhaustible. Mythos is then analysed as the integration of symbol with story and ritual into a comprehensive psychotechnology for activating religio. Finally, the sacred is defined not as a supernatural being but as the transjective relationship between the inexhaustibility of reality (the ever-deeper structure of what is) and the inexhaustibility of RR (the organism's ever-developing capacity for relevance). This definition is intentionally naturalistic, avoiding the category mistake of essentializing relevance into a fixed object.
keyClaims: The symbol has an EPIC structure — Ecstatic, Participatory, Integrative, Complex — each dimension corresponding to a cognitive function in RR.
Polanyi's subsidiary/focal distinction explains how symbols work: the machinery of RR itself becomes the medium of contact with the inexhaustible.
Mythos = symbol + story + ritual: an integrated psychotechnology for activating and sustaining religio.
The sacred is the transjective relationship between the inexhaustibility of reality and the inexhaustibility of RR — not a supernatural entity.
Essentializing relevance — treating the sacred as a fixed property of some object — is the category mistake that generates supernaturalism and eventually its loss.

title: Religio and the Perennial Problems
essence: Episode 36 integrates the two main threads of the series — the historical analysis of the meaning crisis and the cognitive-scientific theory of RR — by introducing the concept of the perennial problems. Vervaeke argues that the meaning crisis is not solely a product of historical contingencies (the Axial Revolution, the Reformation, the Scientific Revolution, the Enlightenment); it is also endemic to the very machinery of RR that makes us adaptive. Because RR is a self-organizing, self-correcting process, it is inherently vulnerable to a set of structural failure modes that cut across cultures and historical periods: parasitic processing (self-reinforcing cycles of self-deception), modal confusion (trying to satisfy being-needs in the having-mode), a reflectiveness gap (the mismatch between knowing and doing), absurdity (the perspectival clash between the first-person sense of meaning and the third-person scientific worldview), anxiety (the confrontation with the contingency of one's existential identity), alienation (the loss of I-thou connectedness to persons and world), and existential entrapment and inertia (being stuck in a worldview one cannot escape and unable to imagine alternatives). Drawing on Frankfurt's analysis of the wanton (one who has no second-order volitions) and Nagel's account of absurdity (the clash between the engaged first-person view and the detached third-person view), Vervaeke stipulatively redefines enlightenment as the reliably achieved developmental process that ameliorates and alleviates the perennial problems — making it a cognitive-scientific target rather than a mystical aspiration.
keyClaims: The perennial problems are structural vulnerabilities of the RR machinery itself — not historically contingent but endemic to being an adaptive meaning-maker.
The seven perennial problems are: parasitic processing, modal confusion, reflectiveness gap, absurdity, anxiety, alienation, and existential entrapment/inertia.
Frankfurt's wanton — one who has no second-order volitions — illustrates the perennial problem of reflectiveness: knowing without being.
Nagel's absurdity — the clash between first-person engagement and third-person detachment — is a perspectival problem, not a logical one, and cannot be resolved by argument alone.
Enlightenment is stipulatively redefined as the reliable amelioration of the perennial problems — a cognitive-scientific, achievable target.

title: Reverse Engineering Enlightenment, Part 2
essence: Episode 37 applies the perennial problems framework systematically by mapping each of the seven problems to a corresponding psychotechnology drawn from the world's wisdom traditions, showing that the traditions independently converged on solutions to the same set of structural vulnerabilities. Parasitic processing is addressed by a counteractive dynamical system — the Buddhist Eightfold Path, which Vervaeke analyses not as a list of rules but as an interlocking set of practices that disrupt self-reinforcing cognitive spirals at multiple points simultaneously. Modal confusion — the confusion of being-needs with having-needs — is addressed by sati (mindfulness in its attentional-scaling sense), which cultivates the capacity to recognize when one is in the having-mode and to shift into the being-mode. The reflectiveness gap — knowing without being — is addressed by flow: the state in which procedural and perspectival knowing are fully integrated with participatory knowing, eliminating the gap between knowing and doing. Absurdity is addressed by prajñā (Buddhist) or scientia intuitiva (Spinoza): a form of non-inferential, participatory knowing that resolves the perspectival clash by changing one's relationship to the conflict rather than arguing one side into silence. Anxiety is addressed by the cultivation of inner dialogue — internalizing the sage in the Stoic manner or the Forms in the Platonic manner — which transforms self-examination from a torment into a resource. Alienation is addressed by communitas (Victor Turner's term for the liminal, egalitarian, anti-structural togetherness that underlies genuine community). Existential entrapment is addressed by gnosis empowered by higher states of consciousness: the participatory knowledge that breaks the worldview lock by actually enacting an alternative salience landscape.
keyClaims: Each perennial problem has a traditional psychotechnology that addresses it: the Eightfold Path for parasitic processing, sati for modal confusion, flow for reflectiveness gap, prajñā/scientia intuitiva for absurdity, inner dialogue for anxiety, communitas for alienation, gnosis/HSC for existential entrapment.

The Eightfold Path is best understood as a counteractive dynamical system: it disrupts parasitic processing at multiple nodes simultaneously, preventing any single practice from being captured by the spiral.
Prajñā and scientia intuitiva are convergent solutions to the same problem: absurdity requires a shift in the mode of knowing, not better arguments within the current mode.
Communitas is Victor Turner's term for the egalitarian, liminal togetherness that underlies authentic community — the psychotechnology for alienation.
These psychotechnologies must be cultivated as an ecology — a mutually supporting set — not as isolated practices.

title: Agape and 4E Cognitive Science
essence: Episode 38 addresses two remaining lacunae in the reverse-engineering project. First, the perennial-problems ecology requires a legitimating worldview — without one, the practices are undermined by the very historical forces that eroded the three orders. Second, Susan Wolf's analysis of meaning in life as subjective attraction meeting objective attractiveness collapses once the scientific worldview denies objective attractiveness; Vervaeke proposes agape as the resolution. Agape is not merely a religious emotion but, in Vervaeke's reframing, the caring about the very conditions that make any caring and any meaning possible — the participatory love of the process of meaning-making itself. Because RR is transjective and self-organizing, the conditions for meaning-making have a value that is not subjective (it does not depend on my valuing them) and not objective in the classical sense (it is not mind-independent), but transjective and, in a Kantian sense, transcendental: they are the conditions of the possibility of meaning. Agape is the orientation in which one cares about those conditions in others, in oneself, in community, and in the world. The episode then turns to 4E cognitive science and Varela's three-point synthesis — embodiment, emergence, emotion — to show how third-generation cognitive science can restore something like the three orders: deep continuity and emergence restore a nomological and normative order, while a post-narrative, open-ended optimization replaces the narrative order. Goodenough's notion of transcendence into (rather than above) frames the metaphysical conclusion: the sacred is found in the depths of nature and psyche, not beyond them.
keyClaims: Agape is the caring about the conditions that make any caring and meaning possible — transjective and transcendental, neither merely subjective nor classically objective.

Wolf's problem of objective attractiveness is resolved not by restoring objectivity but by recognizing that the conditions of meaning-making have a transjective value independent of any individual's valuing.
4E cognitive science — especially Varela's synthesis of embodiment, emergence, and emotion — provides the worldview within which religio, RR, and the ecology of psychotechnologies can be situated.
Deep continuity and emergence through complexification restore a naturalistic nomological and normative order.
Goodenough's transcendence into rather than above reframes the sacred without two-worlds mythology.

title: The Religion of No Religion
essence: Episode 39 moves from the cognitive-scientific diagnosis of the meaning crisis to a constructive, if explicitly tentative, account of what a religion that is not a religion would look like. Vervaeke opens with the concept of religio versus credo: where credo (credal assertion of paradigmatic propositions) has become dominant in Western religion, religio names the higher-order RR that sustains meaning and that credo is only ever supposed to serve. The key normative principle that emerges from signal detection theory is that one must always set a criterion — some orienting framework for determining what counts as genuine religio and what counts as merely going through the motions — but the criterion must never be set absolutely and finally, because absolute credal fixation defeats the open-ended character of religio. The religion that is not a religion therefore requires credo always in the service of religio. Mythos is then analysed across three levels — unconscious (participatory knowing), conscious (perspectival and perspectival knowing), and cultural (distributed cognition) — and shown to be the integrative medium that connects all four kinds of knowing. An ecology of psychotechnologies must be cultivated top-down from the propositional and bottom-up from the participatory in mutual correction. The episode's structural proposal is a meta-psychotechnology — a collectively cultivated capacity for explicitly engineering ecologies of practices — analogous to wisdom at the individual level. The episode concludes by opening the cognitive science of wisdom as the next major topic, anchored in the McKee–Barber synthesis: the philosophical and psychological traditions converge on wisdom as the systematic seeing through self-deception and into reality.
keyClaims: Religio versus credo: credo must always be in the service of religio — the criterion must be set but never set absolutely.

Signal detection theory explains why some criterion is unavoidable: without a framework for distinguishing signal from noise in religio, no practice is possible; but absolute criterion-setting is pathological.
Mythos operates across three levels — unconscious participatory, conscious perspectival, and cultural distributed — and integrates all four kinds of knowing.
A meta-psychotechnology — a collectively cultivated capacity for designing ecologies of psychotechnologies — is the organizational heart of the religion that is not a religion.
The cognitive science of wisdom, anchored in the McKee–Barber convergence argument, identifies wisdom as systematic seeing through self-deception and into reality.

title: Wisdom and Rationality
essence: Vervaeke opens the wisdom arc by interrogating what wisdom actually is, drawing on McKee and Barber's claim that wisdom's central feature is seeing through illusion — the systematic detection and overcoming of self-deception. He argues that wisdom is not defined by its content but by its process: it is a how rather than a what, a manner of knowing rather than a set of propositions to possess. This leads to the insight that wisdom is perspectival and participatory in nature, such that claiming to be wise is a pragmatic self-contradiction — the truly wise person cannot sincerely assert their own wisdom from the outside. Vervaeke then raises the rationality debate: citing Lew Cohen's argument that systematic errors in reasoning are performance rather than competence failures. If people are competent but err through performance limitations, the apparent irrationality of ordinary cognition does not threaten their fundamental rationality. This sets up the distinction between expertise in a domain and general rationality — and why wisdom, which addresses the deepest self-deceptions, cannot be reduced to expertise. The episode frames the arc ahead: a multi-episode investigation of the cognitive science of wisdom.
keyClaims: Wisdom's central feature is the systematic ability to see through illusion and overcome self-deception, not any particular content of knowledge.
Wisdom is a how rather than a what — a process of knowing rather than propositions held, making it inherently perspectival and participatory.
The pragmatic self-contradiction of asserting 'I am wise' demonstrates that wisdom involves a reflexive self-transcendence beyond propositional knowing.
Cohen's competence/performance distinction raises the question of whether systematic reasoning errors are genuine irrationality or situational performance failures.
Expertise in a domain and rationality in the deep sense are categorically different; wisdom requires the latter and cannot be reduced to the former.

title: What is Rationality?
essence: Vervaeke deepens the rationality debate by examining Stanovich and West's response to Cohen. They argue that systematic errors do not merely reflect performance failures but reveal important truths about the nature of competence: competence is not single, not static, and not individual. This leads to an analysis of dual processing theory — the distinction between fast, automatic System 1 and slow, deliberate System 2 — and what it reveals about the layered structure of human cognition. Vervaeke introduces Christopher Cherniak's finitary predicament: any finite reasoner, no matter how rational, faces computational limits that make exhaustive logical consistency checking impossible. This grounds the necessity of relevance realization as the practical engine of rationality. He then examines Smedslund's argument that normative standards in reasoning are not external impositions but are already built into the construal of the problem — rationality is internally constituted. The episode concludes with Baron's notion of active open-mindedness as a key disposition of the rational agent: willingness to seek disconfirming evidence and revise beliefs in response to it.
keyClaims: Competence in reasoning is not single, static, or individual — Stanovich and West show that apparent irrationality often reflects dual-competence conflicts rather than simple error.
The finitary predicament means no finite reasoner can check all logical implications; relevance realization is therefore not optional but constitutive of rationality.
Normative standards in reasoning are not externally imposed but are already constituted in the construal of the problem — rationality cannot be separated from relevance.
Active open-mindedness — the disposition to seek disconfirming evidence and revise beliefs — is a core feature of rationality, not merely a supplement to logic.
The dual processing distinction between System 1 and System 2 reveals that rationality is not a single cognitive capacity but a coordination across multiple levels of processing.

title: Intelligence, Rationality, and Wisdom
essence: Vervaeke develops a recursive hierarchy: intelligence uses rationality, and rationality uses wisdom — each level is needed to properly exercise the level below it. Intelligence is the capacity for flexible problem-solving, but without rationality it misfires through self-deception; rationality is the capacity to systematically overcome self-deception, but without wisdom it cannot navigate the deepest self-deceptions about what matters. This hierarchy is grounded in Stanovich's dual processing account, which is deepened here: System 1 (algorithmic) is fast and automatic while System 2 (reflective) is slow and deliberate, and their coordination requires the kind of cognitive leaping identified by Baker-Sennett and Ceci. Vervaeke also introduces Patricia Arlin's notion of problem finding — the capacity to discover what the real problem is — as a higher-order cognitive ability beyond problem-solving, and frames wonder as the motivational engine of problem finding. Aspiration, via Callard, enters here: rationality is itself aspirational — one must aspire to become rational. The episode culminates in the claim that intelligence and logicality are categorically distinct from rationality, which in turn is distinct from wisdom.
keyClaims: Intelligence, rationality, and wisdom form a recursive hierarchy: each higher level is needed to properly exercise the level below it — wisdom governs rationality, rationality governs intelligence.
Problem finding — discovering what the real problem is — is a higher-order cognitive ability than problem-solving, and wonder is its motivational engine.
Active open-mindedness and mindfulness are opponent cognitive styles: one counteracts motivated reasoning, the other suspends judgment; wisdom requires both in appropriate proportion.
Aspiration is constitutive of rationality, not supplementary — one aspires to rationality from a position that is not yet fully rational, making proleptic rationality irreducible.
Intelligence does not entail rationality, and rationality does not entail logicality; the conflation of these is a persistent cultural error that wisdom must correct.

title: Wisdom and Virtue
essence: Vervaeke examines wisdom through the lens of virtue ethics, exploring the claim that wisdom is not merely an intellectual capacity but a moral and character achievement. He engages Schwartz and Sharpe's critique of rule-following approaches to ethics: virtues conflict, their application is context-sensitive, and their development requires a cultivation that cannot be captured by expertise. Phronesis — practical wisdom — is distinguished from theoretical sophia: the former navigates the particular situation, the latter ascends to universal principles. Vervaeke argues that the expertise model of virtue is fundamentally mistaken because virtue conflicts require the perspectival and participatory knowing of the particular situation, not the application of domain-specific knowledge. He then surveys the Berlin wisdom paradigm of Baltes and Staudinger, with its five criteria: rich factual knowledge, rich procedural knowledge, lifespan contextualism, value relativism (recast as fallibilism), and the management of uncertainty. Vervaeke critiques this paradigm as measuring wisdom's product rather than its process, and notes that Igor Grossmann's Solomon effect — we are wiser about others' problems than our own — reveals the crucial role of perspectival distance. The episode closes noting that platonic dialogue itself is a psychotechnology for developing the perspectival distance that wisdom requires.
keyClaims: Virtue conflicts cannot be resolved by expertise in any single domain; they require practical wisdom — phronesis — which involves perspectival and participatory knowing of the particular situation.
The Berlin wisdom paradigm (Baltes and Staudinger) captures wisdom's product — rich factual and procedural knowledge with lifespan contextualism — but not its underlying process.
The Solomon effect demonstrates that we exercise greater wisdom about others' problems than our own, revealing the constitutive role of perspectival distance in wisdom.
Schwartz and Sharpe's critique shows that virtue cannot be reduced to rule-following or expertise; it requires the cultivated perception of what matters in a situation.
Platonic dialogue is a psychotechnology that develops perspectival distance and thereby enhances wisdom by enabling one to take a third-person view on one's own situation.

title: Theories of Wisdom
essence: Vervaeke continues the survey of wisdom theories, beginning with further critique of the Berlin paradigm and then examining Ardelt's three-factor model and Sternberg's balance theory. The Berlin paradigm is critiqued for confusing wisdom with expertise: it describes knowledge states but not the transformative process by which those states come to be, and its practitioners often look like domain experts rather than genuinely wise persons. Ardelt's model identifies three interacting components — cognitive (understanding causes and significance), reflective (perspectival self-examination), and affective (agape, compassionate concern for others) — and Vervaeke argues this is a significant advance because it connects wisdom to meaning in life through agape. John Kekes's distinction between descriptive and interpretive knowledge is introduced: interpretive knowledge is not just factual knowledge about the world but a way of seeing the deep patterns that link facts to human significance. Sternberg's balance theory frames wisdom as the application of tacit understanding to balance intrapersonal, interpersonal, and extrapersonal interests — adapt, shape, or select environments — toward a common good. LA Paul's transformative experience is cited as illuminating why this balance cannot be achieved through deliberation alone. The episode closes with the claim that any adequate wisdom theory must account for gnosis — the transformative participatory knowing that restructures the self.
keyClaims: The Berlin wisdom paradigm confuses wisdom with expertise by focusing on knowledge products rather than the transformative cognitive process that generates them.

Ardelt's three-factor model — cognitive understanding, reflective perspectival examination, and agape — is a significant advance because it connects wisdom to meaning in life through compassionate concern.
Kekes's interpretive knowledge — the ability to see how facts bear on human significance — is a necessary component of wisdom beyond mere factual knowledge.
Sternberg's balance theory frames wisdom as tacit understanding used to balance intrapersonal, interpersonal, and extrapersonal interests toward a common good.
An adequate theory of wisdom must account for gnosis — the participatory knowing that transforms the self — which no purely propositional account can capture.

title: The Nature of Wisdom
essence: This episode delivers the capstone of Vervaeke's theory of wisdom, presenting his 2013 model developed with Leo Ferraro. The model identifies three tiers of interacting capacities: at the inferential level, wisdom requires active open-mindedness to counteract motivated reasoning; at the insight level, mindfulness cultivates the attentional flexibility needed to break inappropriate frames; and at the perspectival level, wisdom involves the internalization of a sage — an imaginal model of a wiser self whose perspective restructures one's salience landscape. Sophrosyne is analyzed as the optimal expression of perspectival knowing: the condition in which one's salience landscape is so well cultivated that it naturally tracks the good, as distinguished from enkratia, in which one must effortfully override contrary impulses. The three M's of wisdom are introduced — morality, meaning in life, and mastery — as the domains in which wisdom is applied. Aspiration is given its full role via Callard's proleptic rationality: wisdom is not a state one arrives at but a process one aspires through. Understanding is reconceived as optimal grip plus problem finding, and profound understanding as the plausibility generation that aligns with all four kinds of knowing. The episode culminates in the claim that wisdom is a dynamical system — self-organizing and counteractive to the machinery of self-deception — that affords transformation into the life of flourishing.
keyClaims: Wisdom is a dynamical system integrating active open-mindedness, insight, and perspectival internalization of a sage, operating at inferential, insight, and perspectival levels respectively.
Sophrosyne is the optimal form of perspectival knowing: the salience landscape so cultivated that virtue is natural rather than requiring effortful enkratic override.
Profound understanding involves optimal grip plus problem finding across all four kinds of knowing — propositional, procedural, perspectival, and participatory.
Wisdom is aspirational and proleptic: one aspires to wisdom from a position not yet wise, and that very aspiration must be wise to avoid self-refutation.

Gnosis — transformative participatory knowing — is necessary for a complete account of wisdom; without it, wisdom remains a product theory that cannot explain the self-organized transformation it produces.

title: Conclusion and the Prophets of the Meaning Crisis
essence: Vervaeke integrates the wisdom account with his earlier theory of enlightenment and religio, proposing Wise Cultivation of Enlightenment (WCE) as the practical response to the meaning crisis: a cooperative network of communities of practice, embedded within a 4E cognitive science worldview, supported by a wisdom wiki, and engaged in ongoing dialogue with the prophets of the meaning crisis. He draws a detailed roadmap of those prophets — with Heidegger at the center, connected to Tillich, to Jung and Corbin through the gnosis underground, to Barfield through the Romantics, and to speculative realists (Harman) and the Kyoto school (Nishitani) through adjacent movements. The episode then turns to Husserl and phenomenology: intentionality (noesis-noema structure) as the precursor to Heidegger's ontological project, and Vervaeke draws the parallel between the noesis/noema correlation and the agent-arena relationship. He then explains Heidegger's core criticism of Husserl: phenomenology fails to adequately ground participatory knowing in ontology — it remains trapped in Cartesian subjectivity. Merleau-Ponty is credited with partially addressing this through embodiment. The notion of Dasein is introduced: we are the being whose being is in question, and by questing into our own being, we come into contact with being itself.
keyClaims: Wise Cultivation of Enlightenment (WCE) integrates the wisdom account with religio to propose a practical response to the meaning crisis: a cooperative network of communities of practice within a 4E worldview.
Heidegger occupies the center of the prophets diagram, with Tillich, Jung, Corbin, Barfield, speculative realists, and the Kyoto school as connected nodes.
Husserl's phenomenology (noesis-noema intentional structure) is a partial recovery of contact epistemology but remains trapped in Cartesian subjectivity.

Heidegger's criticism of Husserl is that phenomenology fails to adequately ground participatory knowing in ontology — the agent-arena relationship is not yet set within a deeper understanding of being.
Dasein — the being whose being is in question — is Heidegger's starting point: by questing into our own being, we simultaneously quest into the mystery of being itself.

title: Heidegger
essence: Vervaeke examines Heidegger's philosophical project as a prophetic response to the meaning crisis. The central thread is Heidegger's critique of the history of metaphysics as the history of nihilism — the systematic forgetfulness of being that results from becoming trapped in the having mode. From Heidegger's essay on the essence of truth, Vervaeke draws the key insight that truth as correctness (correspondence between statements and reality) is grounded in a deeper attunement — a comportment toward things that makes the agent-arena relationship possible in the first place. This attunement is identified with relevance realization, and its forgetfulness is identified as modal confusion. The essay on Angelus Silesius's rose poem illustrates the alternative: physis, the self-grounding blossoming of things that simultaneously shine into our experience and withdraw beyond our framing — the thing beyond itself. Truth as aletheia — unconcealment, a deep remembering that is simultaneously a disclosure of things shining and withdrawing — is the response to forgetfulness. Dreyfus is identified as the interpreter who connects Heidegger's thought to 4E cognitive science through the notion of optimal grip. Corbin's reading of Heidegger as anticipating gnosis — the transformative participatory knowing in which self-knowledge and world-knowledge are coupled — is introduced. The problem of onto-theology — understanding being in terms of a supreme being — is identified as the metaphysical error that deepens nihilism.
keyClaims: The history of metaphysics is the history of nihilism: a systematic forgetfulness of being produced by modal confusion — getting trapped in the having mode and forgetting the being mode.
Truth as correspondence between statements and reality is grounded in a deeper attunement that Heidegger calls the essence of truth — aletheia, an unconcealment that must be actively remembered.
The thing beyond itself — simultaneously shining into experience and withdrawing from our framing — is the ontological structure that grounds both relevance realization and sacredness.
Onto-theology — understanding being in terms of a supreme being — is the modal confusion that deepens nihilism by trying to capture the inexhaustible in a propositional object.
Corbin reads Heidegger as anticipating gnosis: the transformative participatory knowing where self-knowledge and world-knowledge are coupled in a dynamical conformity.

title: Corbin and the Divine Double
essence: Vervaeke turns to Henry Corbin as the thinker who develops Heidegger's gnosis through the specific resources of Persian Sufism and Neoplatonism. Beginning with Heidegger's commentary on the rose poem of Angelus Silesius, Vervaeke draws the contrast between narrative teleology and vertical ontology: the rose blooms without why, grounding itself in itself, and this non-teleological being mode is what physis means. This is exapted from narrative into a vertical movement from the shallower frame of the current self toward the depths of being. Corbin's central contribution is the distinction between the imaginary (subjective mental imagery, merely possessed) and the imaginal: the transjective use of image that mediates between the abstract intelligible world and the concrete sensible world, and between subjective and objective — in an ongoing transformative transframing. The symbol in Corbin's sense is translucent, transjective, trajective, transformative, and transtemporal. Fundamentalism and literalism destroy the imaginal by collapsing it into either the abstract or the concrete, ending gnosis. The divine double is then introduced: the motif prevalent across Hellenistic Mediterranean spirituality (Stang's scholarship) of a superlative self to which one's current self is bound and toward which one aspires. The divine double as imaginal symbol allows one to internalize a more encompassing frame into one's current frame (the shining in) while being drawn toward the indwelling of that larger world (the withdrawing). LA Paul's transformative experience and Callard's aspiration are brought in as the analytic philosophy of the same process.
keyClaims: Corbin distinguishes the imaginary (subjective mental imagery) from the imaginal: the transjective use of image that mediates between abstract intelligible and concrete sensible worlds in an ongoing transformative transframing.
Fundamentalism and literalism are pernicious precisely because they collapse the imaginal into either the abstract or the concrete, destroying the capacity for gnosis.

The divine double is the Hellenistic-Mediterranean motif of a superlative self to which one is bound and toward which one aspires — transgressive of the romantic grammar that treats the true self as something possessed.
The symbol in Corbin's sense is simultaneously translucent, transjective, trajective, transformative, and transtemporal — the cognitive medium through which gnosis operates.
Corbin connects Heidegger's attunement to gnosis by showing that the imaginal is the mode through which one remembers being in aletheia — recovering the being mode from forgetfulness.

title: Corbin and Jung
essence: Vervaeke uses Callard's aspiration account to deepen the analysis of the divine double before pivoting to Jung. The S1-to-S2 aspiration involves a fundamental paradox: S1 causally produces S2 but is normatively dependent on S2 — the first self causes the second but only the second justifies the first. Galen Strawson's paradox of self-creation is introduced to sharpen this: genuine self-creation requires both continuity (same self) and novelty (genuinely new), yet these requirements are incompatible. Callard's resolution is that S1 does not make nor receive S2 but participates in S2's emergence — a non-logical identity that narrative practice trains us to tolerate. The divine double is reinterpreted in this light as an imaginal symbol enabling the dynamic coupling of anagoge: it shines the greater frame into the current frame while drawing one out into indwelling that frame. Vervaeke renames the divine double the sacred second self to separate the concept from theism. This transition leads to Jung, whose notion of individuation is introduced as the psychological account of the sacred second self process. Jung's model of the psyche as a self-organizing autopoietic system — contrasted with Freud's hydraulic model — grounds the archetypes as imaginal virtual engines of self-organization rather than static images. Vervaeke's key criticism of Jung follows Corbin and Buber: Jung reduces the imaginal to the merely intra-psychic, losing the transjective world-disclosing dimension. Jung, in turn, supplies what Corbin lacks: a detailed psychology of the internalization process.
keyClaims: In aspiration, S1 is temporally prior but normatively secondary to S2: the first self causes the second, but only the second justifies and explains the first — an irreducible normative reversal.

Strawson's paradox of self-creation is resolved by Callard's notion that S1 participates in S2's emergence rather than making or receiving it — a non-logical identity that resists both empiricist and romantic reductions.
The divine double, renamed the sacred second self to separate it from theism, is the imaginal symbol that enables anagogic dynamic coupling between the current frame and the more encompassing one.
Jung's archetypes are imaginal virtual engines of psychic self-organization — not static images to possess but ongoing structural processes that reorganize salience and participatory being.
Jung's main weakness is his Kantian intra-psychic reduction of the imaginal: he loses the transjective world-disclosure that Corbin preserves, while he gains a detailed psychology that Corbin lacks.

title: Tillich and Barfield
essence: The series concludes with Tillich and Barfield, the final two prophets. Tillich's response to the meaning crisis centers on existential courage — not mere bravery or endurance, but the virtue of seeing through illusion to the good and acting accordingly. His notion of faith as ultimate concern is participatory and aspirational: not asserting propositions but entering into an aspirational trajectory toward the inexhaustible ground of being. For Tillich, God is not a supreme being but an imaginal icon for the ground of being — the simultaneous ground of meaning, reality, and their relationship — such that any attempt to literalize or reify God is idolatry. His method of correlation is not propositional theology but anagogic resonance between the depths of reason (ekstasis) and the depths of reality (miracle as shining and mystery as withdrawal) — coupled together through the symbol. Tillich's existential self/essential self distinction maps precisely onto Vervaeke's sacred second self. Barfield then offers the recovery of participation through poiesis: the ekstasis within creativity, grounded in the Romantic insight (Schlegel/Coleridge) that the finite longs for the infinite. Final participation — recovery of original participation integrated with rational science — requires both Gelassenheit (Heidegger's letting be) and Durchbruch (breakthrough). Vervaeke closes the series by affirming that his relevance realization framework has been put into deep dialogue with all five prophets, yielding a potential synoptic integration.
keyClaims: Tillich's courage to be is an existential virtue involving seeing through illusion — connecting wisdom, religio, and the response to meaninglessness as despair.
Faith as ultimate concern is participatory and aspirational: not propositional assertion but an anagogic trajectory toward the inexhaustible ground of being.

God as the ground of being — an imaginal icon, not a supreme being — survives the critique of ontotheology; literalizing God is idolatry that destroys the symbolic mediation through which gnosis operates.

Barfield's final participation — recovering original participation within the gains of rational science — requires both Gelassenheit (letting be) and Durchbruch (breakthrough), pointing toward the creative poiesis of ekstasis.

The relevance realization framework can enter deep dialogue with all five prophets — Heidegger, Corbin, Jung, Tillich, Barfield — potentially affording a synoptic integration as the response to the meaning crisis.
